title: ENGLAND: FROM A TWO-PARTY SYSTEM TO A FIVE-PARTY ONE
body_paragraphs: AI SUMMARY GENERATED ONLY FROM THIS ARTICLE'S TEXT.
Reform UK Leader Nigel Farage speaks next to newly elected councilors at the Havering Town Hall, following the results of the local elections in London, May 8, 2026

Going by recent media analyses, some Sri Lankan commentators seem to think that gains by England’s far right Reform Party, and losses by the ruling Labour Party, point to Nigel Farage’s Reform being the dominant force in British politics from now on. But a closer look at elections results shows that this isn’t a foregone conclusion. At first glance, the results of English Council elections held on May 07 look shocking. Labour lost more than half of its council seats, while Reform was the main beneficiary with more than 600 new councillors. But there were three more parties in the contention. What was overlooked in the initial excitement was that England’s familiar two-party system had changed in the short span of one year to a five-party system. The three other parties are the Conservatives, the Greens, and the Liberal Democrats. While the Conservatives lost badly, the Green Party made significant gains, and Liberal Democrats too, progressed, though not dramatically.
The cracks in the two-party system

While Reform UK stood out as the main beneficiary, the Greens and the Liberal Democrats too, got plenty of votes, and both these parties are left-ofcentre. The cracks in the two-party system have been evident for some time, with the combined Labour and Conservative share of the vote rarely above 40%, and the Green party challenging for second place. Now Reform has become top dog, but that doesn’t mean the other parties are knocked out of the contention. It looks like the five-party system is here to stay. In any case, it is hard to see how Reform can find the money to carry out proposed sweeping reforms in England’s national health care system. Making promises is easier than keeping them. Traditional political landscapes can shift dramatically overnight. In Sri Lanka, though we talk of a two-party system, there have always been smaller parties in the fray. In the pre-1978 era, left wing parties such as the Communist Party and Sama Samaja Party had enough clout to form or topple governments. In the post-1978, right wing parties such as Jathika Hela Urumaya played significant roles. The dramatic emergence of National People’s Power in the last general election, itself an amalgamation of what had been till then two also-ran parties, is a good example of these sudden seismic shifts in political tectonic plates. Western Europe gives us plenty of examples of such dark horse parties making significant gains over the past decade, even forming governments. Most of these parties are centre-right or far right. But their rise is not unstoppable. France came precariously close to having a far right government in 2017, when Marine Le Pen looked unstoppable. But the French electorate chose centrist Emmanuel Macron. Despite its ferocity, the National Front Party of Marine Le Pen (Le Front National) was a marginal party till that point. But, even though Macron won the election with 66% of the vote, Le Front National went mainstream with 33% of the vote. In some provincial elections, socialist party candidates withdrew voluntarily, letting their centrist opponents defeat Le Pen and win. Both the socialists and the conservatives view the far right as their common enemy. But the socialists have been clearly relegated to the backseat since the 2007 presidential elections, when socialist candidate Segolene Royal and rightwing candidate Nicolas Sarkozy faced off each other. While Sarkozy became president with 53% of the vote, Royal managed to get 46%. The socialists haven’t regained this electoral position since. But Sarkozy was discredited and jailed in 2025 (as former president), accused of receiving money from former Libyan leader Muammar Gaddafi to finance his 2007 election campaign. Sarkozy wasn’t as far to the right as Le Pen, but one can argue that such politicians are spearheading a right-wing resurgence in Western Europe. As with England’s Reform, Le Pen’s party too, functions from an anti-immigrant policy. In the French case, many disgruntled former socialists became far right supporters. Something similar may be happening in England, too, but this does not mean that the far right is unstoppable. Perhaps we should go back to the Europe of 1930s for a comparison, when the picture looked even more sinister and complex and fascism looked unstoppable. Spain’s left-wing government was defeated by fascist forces in the Spanish civil war (1936-39), heralding World War II and posing the biggest threat faced by liberal democracies in the 20th century. Hitler and Mussolini are the most recognisable faces of European fascism and Nazism. But in every Western European country, including England, France and the Nordic states, there were large numbers of fascists and Nazi sympathisers ready to welcome invasion and regime change. Like most of Europe, Britain had suffered cataclysmic change since the 1920s – the Great Depression, followed by the crippling general strike of 1926. The Conservative government of Stanley Baldwin used armoured cars to move food convoys, and the strike ended with workers disillusioned and coal miners feeling betrayed when the Trade Union Congress called off the strike. The Conservatives lost to Ramsay McDonald’s Labour at the 1929 general elections. But politics had become very volatile with a worsening economy after the Wall Street crash of 1929, which caused the new Labour government’s downfall. A national government was formed with McDonald as prime minister but just three Labour Party members. When World War II broke out, it’s Conservative Neville Chamberlain who headed the National government. He is remembered for betraying Czechoslovakia in the infamous ‘Peace of Our Time.’ But happily, British fascist and Nazi groups were discredited and marginalised by this time. The British Union of Fascists (BUF) led by Oswald Mosley labelled themselves a peace movement in order to survive, and openly pro-Nazi factions like The Link and the Imperial Fascist League were tiny, fringe organisations. In France, the situation was more volatile. There was a right-wing coalition in power as far back as 1919-24. The Republic was politically unstable, with constantly changing coalition governments. A shortlived leftwing coalition was replaced by a rightwing coalition (Union Nationale) from 1926-32. But France remained plagued by economic problems. In 1936, the famous Front Populaire, a socialistcommunist coalition came to power with Leon Blum as prime minister. It managed long overdue reforms but was brought down by another financial crisis in 1938. After the German conquest, the French fascists began collaborating openly with the Nazis, and even some of the communists and socialists became ‘pacifists.’ The major difference now and then was that, apart from the leftists, Jews were seen as the principal enemies. Today, the main enemies of the European right are non-white immigrants. In the 1930s, the hate was both political and racial. Today, it is almost entirely racial.
